title: Setup Surfaces and Permissions Check
summary: Confirm the learner understands when to use each surface and how to stay safe.

questions.prompt: Which surface is the best default starting point for repo-aware builder work?
options: Claude Code in the terminal
A browser tab with no files
A slide deck
A blank text editor
questions.explanation: CLI Academy treats Claude Code in the terminal as the safest default place to learn repo-aware builder workflows.

questions.prompt: What should a beginner do before approving a file-changing action?
options: Approve immediately to save time
Review what will change and why
Restart the machine
Open a new browser tab
questions.explanation: The safe default is to inspect the requested action, scope, and expected output before approval.

questions.prompt: What does the YES gate in Claude Code do?
options: Approves all actions automatically
Asks for your confirmation before executing certain tool calls
Blocks Claude from running any commands
Sends data to Anthropic for review
questions.explanation: The YES gate pauses execution before state-changing tool calls and shows you exactly what Claude intends to do so you can approve or deny.

questions.prompt: Which action requires the most caution before approval?
options: Asking Claude to explain a file
Having Claude delete or overwrite multiple files
Running claude --version
Reading a README with Claude
questions.explanation: Destructive operations like deleting or overwriting files are hard to reverse. Always review the scope and diff before approving.

questions.prompt: Why run claude --version before starting each session?
options: It is required — Claude will not work otherwise
To confirm the CLI is installed and note the version for troubleshooting
To reset Claude's memory
To check your API balance
questions.explanation: Checking the version before each session confirms your tooling is ready and gives you a concrete version number if you need to report a bug or find a changelog entry.

title: Prompt Anatomy Check
summary: Check clarity, constraints, examples, and output structure understanding.

questions.prompt: Which prompt is usually stronger for a beginner workflow?
options: Do something smart with this repo
Summarize this repo in 5 bullets and suggest one safe first improvement
Fix everything
Use your best judgment
questions.explanation: Specific outcome, format, and scope almost always outperform vague prompting.

questions.prompt: Which element is most likely missing from the prompt 'Fix this code'?
options: A task type label
Context about which code and what is broken
A word count limit
A model selection flag
questions.explanation: 'Fix this code' has no context — which code? What is broken? What does correct output look like? Context is the most commonly missing element.

questions.prompt: What does adding an example to a prompt usually accomplish?
options: It slows Claude down
It calibrates format and style without over-constraining the output
It makes Claude ignore other instructions
It reduces the token count
questions.explanation: Examples show Claude the pattern you want without narrowing it to only that exact output. They are one of the most effective prompt techniques available.

questions.prompt: Why is specifying output format often more useful than describing tone?
options: Tone is always easy to adjust after the fact
Format is verifiable — you can check whether a table has the right columns or a list has the right length
Claude cannot understand tone instructions
Format reduces API cost
questions.explanation: A concrete format (3-column table, numbered list, one-page summary) is machine-verifiable. Tone is subjective and harder to validate reliably.

questions.prompt: What is the safest first prompt when approaching an unfamiliar codebase?
options: Add a new feature immediately
Ask Claude to explain the top-level structure before touching anything
Delete the files that look unused
Run the test suite and see what breaks
questions.explanation: Understand before acting. Explore first, change second. This habit prevents accidental damage and gives Claude the context it needs to make safe suggestions.

title: Cowork Task Framing Check
summary: Confirm that the learner can turn vague knowledge-work requests into structured deliverables.

questions.prompt: Which instruction is better for Cowork?
options: Look through these files and do something useful
Review this folder and produce a 1-page briefing with 3 risks, 3 actions, and a clean summary table
Use your instincts
Write a bunch of notes
questions.explanation: Cowork performs best when the deliverable, structure, and expected output are explicit.

questions.prompt: What is a session brief in Claude Cowork?
options: A short summary Claude writes at the end of a session
A template you write at the start of a session to orient Claude on what you need
A billing record of Claude usage
A document Claude emails to you after the session
questions.explanation: A session brief is a short structured prompt you write before the first substantive message. It tells Claude what you need, what good looks like, and what to avoid — so you don't have to re-orient on every turn.

questions.prompt: Which instruction produces the most immediately usable summary document?
options: Summarize things for me
Write a 1-page executive summary with a context section, 3 key findings, and a recommended next action
Tell me what you know
Review my files and be helpful
questions.explanation: Specifying page length, sections, and expected output gives Claude a concrete target. Vague instructions produce vague output that requires many follow-up edits.

questions.prompt: What do 'negative constraints' mean in a Cowork session brief?
options: Telling Claude what to avoid, such as passive voice or bullet-point overuse
Asking Claude to produce fewer words
Disabling Claude's memory for the session
Blocking Claude from reading files
questions.explanation: Negative constraints are explicit 'do NOT' instructions. Claude follows them reliably and they are the fastest way to cut common output anti-patterns like marketing filler, excessive bullets, or passive voice.

questions.prompt: When should you upload source documents to a Cowork session?
options: Never — Claude should work from general knowledge
When the task requires grounding in specific files, data, transcripts, or contracts
Only for PDF files
Only when Claude explicitly asks for them
questions.explanation: Upload source documents whenever the task requires working with specific content rather than general knowledge. Claude can read PDFs, Word docs, spreadsheets, and plain text dragged directly into the chat.

title: Runtime Basics Check
summary: Check support-tier judgment, setup burden awareness, and safer default runtime selection.

questions.prompt: What is the safest default for a beginner's first runtime?
options: The most experimental runtime because it is exciting
A runtime with stronger support tier and higher beginner fit
Any runtime with the fewest configuration files
Whichever runtime has the shortest setup guide
questions.explanation: CLI Academy teaches beginners to prefer stronger support and clearer debugging loops over novelty.

questions.prompt: What does 'support tier' indicate for a runtime?
options: How much the runtime costs to run per month
How reliable and battle-tested the runtime is across real deployments
How many concurrent users the runtime can support
The runtime's maximum token throughput
questions.explanation: Support tier (experimental, emerging, stable) tells you how confident you should be in the runtime's stability and how much community help you will find if something goes wrong.

questions.prompt: What does high setup complexity mean for a beginner?
options: The output quality of Claude will be higher
More steps, more dependencies, and more surfaces where setup can fail
The runtime uses more memory
The number of skills you can install is unlimited
questions.explanation: High setup complexity translates directly to more time debugging installation issues before you can do real work. Beginners should prefer low-complexity runtimes to keep the learning loop tight.

questions.prompt: When is it appropriate to choose an experimental runtime?
options: For your very first agentic project
When you need cutting-edge features and are comfortable debugging with limited support
When you want the safest, most predictable experience
When you need the best beginner documentation
questions.explanation: Experimental runtimes offer new capabilities but come with less stability and less community support. They are appropriate for experienced developers who are comfortable working at the frontier.

questions.prompt: What is the primary trade-off ZeroClaw makes compared to OpenClaw?
options: More configuration options but slower startup
Lower latency by stripping the gateway layer, at the cost of skill execution and security features
Better security hardening but higher setup complexity
More skill compatibility but no web interface
questions.explanation: ZeroClaw removes the OpenClaw gateway to reduce round-trip latency. This trades flexibility, skill execution, and the YES gate for raw speed — useful when you do not need the full feature set.
